explanation_en: Use '了' at the end of a sentence to indicate that an action has been completed. This is the basic past tense marker in Chinese.
title: Completed Action with 了
meaning: Indicates an action is completed
when_to_use: When talking about something you finished doing
When reporting what happened
When answering 'what did you do?'
when_not_to_use: When describing habitual actions
When talking about future plans
When using negated past actions (use 没有 instead)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 了 is commonly used in casual conversation to confirm completion. It's less formal than using 已經 (already).
word_order_notes: 了 always comes at the end of the sentence, after the object. Do not place it between the verb and object.
english_contrast: English uses past tense verb forms (e.g., 'ate', 'drank'), while Chinese adds 了 at the end of the sentence.
korean_contrast: Korean uses '-었/았다' attached to the verb stem, while Chinese uses 了 at the end of the sentence.

negative_examples.error: This sentence is correct. No error.

incorrect_examples.error: Time word '昨天' should come before the verb, not at the end.
Time word '昨天' should come before the verb, not after the object.

transformation_drills: Change '我吃早餐' to past tense using 了.
Change '他看電影' to past tense using 了.
production_drills: Tell your partner what you ate for breakfast yesterday.
Tell your partner what you did yesterday afternoon.
common_error_patterns: Placing 了 after the verb instead of at the end
Using 了 with 没有 in negative sentences

repair_feedback.word_order: Remember: 了 goes at the end of the sentence, not after the verb.
repair_feedback.politeness: This structure is neutral in politeness.
repair_feedback.vocabulary: Use action verbs like 吃, 喝, 看, 去 with this pattern.

pattern: 昨天 / 上個禮拜 / 上個月 + Subject + Verb + Object + 了
explanation_en: Use a past time word at the beginning of the sentence to specify when the action happened. Common time words include 昨天 (yesterday), 上個禮拜 (last week), 上個月 (last month), 剛才 (just now).
title: Past Time Words with 了
meaning: Specifies when a past action occurred
when_to_use: When you want to specify the time of a past action
When telling a story about yesterday or last week
When answering 'when did you do that?'
when_not_to_use: When the time is obvious from context
When talking about future events
When using 今天 (today) for actions not yet completed
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 昨天 is very common in daily conversation. You can also use 上個禮拜 for last week or 上個月 for last month.
word_order_notes: Time word comes first in the sentence, before the subject. This is different from English where time can come at the end.
english_contrast: English can place time words at the beginning or end (e.g., 'I ate breakfast yesterday' or 'Yesterday I ate breakfast'). Chinese requires time words at the beginning.
korean_contrast: Korean places time words before the verb but after the subject (e.g., '나는 어제 아침을 먹었다'), while Chinese places time words at the very beginning.

negative_examples.error: This sentence is correct. No error.

incorrect_examples.error: Time word '昨天' must come at the beginning of the sentence, not at the end.
Missing 了 to indicate the action is completed.

transformation_drills: Add '昨天' to the sentence '我喝咖啡了'.
Change '我上個禮拜看電影了' to use '昨天'.
production_drills: Tell your partner what you did last week.
Tell your partner what you did yesterday morning.
common_error_patterns: Placing time word at the end of the sentence
Forgetting 了 when using a past time word

repair_feedback.word_order: Time word must come first, before the subject.
repair_feedback.politeness: This structure is neutral in politeness.
repair_feedback.vocabulary: Use time words like 昨天, 上個禮拜, 上個月, 剛才.

pattern: Subject + Verb + Object + 了，然後 + Subject + Verb + Object + 了
explanation_en: Use 然後 (then) to connect two completed actions in sequence. Both actions should end with 了 to indicate they are completed.
title: Sequencing Past Actions with 然後
meaning: Describes a sequence of past events
when_to_use: When telling a story about what you did in order
When describing your daily routine yesterday
When explaining a series of past actions
when_not_to_use: When actions happened at the same time
When describing future plans
When using 先 (first) and 再 (then) for future actions
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 然後 is very common in spoken Chinese to connect events. It's similar to 'and then' in English.
word_order_notes: Each clause follows the Subject + Verb + Object + 了 pattern. 然後 connects the two clauses.
english_contrast: English uses 'and then' or 'then' to connect past actions. Chinese uses 然後 between two clauses, each ending with 了.
korean_contrast: Korean uses '-고' or '-서' to connect sequential actions (e.g., '먹고 갔다'), while Chinese uses 然後 between separate clauses.

correct_examples.text: 我昨天起床了，然後刷牙了。
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ zuótiān qǐchuáng le, ránhòu shuāyá le.
correct_examples.translation_en: I got up yesterday, and then I brushed my teeth.

correct_examples.text: 我昨天吃早餐了，然後出門了。
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ zuótiān chī zǎocān le, ránhòu chūmén le.
correct_examples.translation_en: I ate breakfast yesterday, and then I went out.

correct_examples.text: 我昨天上班了，然後下班了。

explanation_en: Use 然後 to connect two past events in chronological order, meaning 'and then' or 'after that'.
title: Sequencing with 然後
meaning: Connecting two events in order
when_to_use: When telling a story about what happened step by step
When describing your daily routine or a past sequence
when_not_to_use: When events happen at the same time
When you want to show cause and effect (use 因為...所以)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 然後 is very common in spoken narratives. You can use it multiple times in a row to keep the story going.
word_order_notes: 然後 always comes between the two events. The first event is stated, then 然後, then the second event.
english_contrast: English uses 'and then' or 'after that'. Chinese uses 然後 in the same position between clauses.
korean_contrast: Korean uses 그리고 (geurigo) or 그 다음에 (geu daeume) in a similar way, placed between clauses.

pattern: 先 A，然後 B，再 C
explanation_en: Use 先...然後...再... to list three or more steps in order, meaning 'first A, then B, then C'.
title: Multiple Steps with 先...然後...再...
meaning: Listing a sequence of actions
when_to_use: When describing a process with three or more steps
When telling a story with a clear beginning, middle, and end
when_not_to_use: When there are only two steps (use just 然後)
When the order is not important
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, this pattern is very common for giving instructions or telling a story. You can add more steps by repeating 然後 or 再.
word_order_notes: 先 comes before the first action, 然後 before the second, 再 before the third. Do not put 先 after the first action.
english_contrast: English uses 'first...then...then...' or 'first...after that...finally'. Chinese uses 先...然後...再... in the same order.
korean_contrast: Korean uses 먼저...그리고...그 다음에... (meonjeo...geurigo...geu daeume...) in a similar pattern.

explanation_en: Place 了 after the verb to indicate a completed past action. This is a review of the basic past tense structure.
title: Past Action with 了 (Review)
meaning: Indicating a completed action
when_to_use: When talking about something that happened and finished in the past
When answering 'what did you do?' questions
when_not_to_use: When describing habitual actions (use present tense)
When the action is ongoing or not yet completed
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 了 is very common in spoken Chinese for past events. However, for verbs like 是, 有, 在, 了 is not used.
word_order_notes: 了 goes immediately after the verb, before the object. Do not put 了 at the end of the sentence if there is an object.
english_contrast: English uses past tense (e.g., 'ate'). Chinese uses 了 after the verb. No change in verb form.
korean_contrast: Korean uses -었/았다 (-eot/atda) at the end of the verb. Chinese places 了 after the verb but before the object.

explanation_en: Use 過 (guò) after a verb to indicate that someone has experienced or done something at least once in the past. It emphasizes the experience itself, not when it happened.
title: Expressing Past Experience with 過
meaning: Have done something before
when_to_use: When asking if someone has ever done something
When sharing personal experiences or travel stories
When talking about things you have tried or visited
when_not_to_use: Do not use 過 for actions that happened at a specific time (use 了 instead)
Do not use 過 for ongoing or habitual actions
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 過 is very common in casual conversation, especially when talking about travel (去過台北), food (吃過滷肉飯), or experiences (看過那部電影). It's neutral and polite.
word_order_notes: Place 過 directly after the verb and before the object. The word order is: Subject + Verb + 過 + Object.
english_contrast: In English, we use 'have/has + past participle' (e.g., 'I have been to Taipei'). In Chinese, 過 replaces the auxiliary verb 'have' and is attached to the main verb.
korean_contrast: In Korean, the equivalent is '-아/어 본 적이 있다' (e.g., '저는 타이베이에 가 본 적이 있어요'). 過 is simpler and directly attached to the verb.

negative_examples.error: This sentence uses 過 with a specific time (昨天), which is incorrect. 過 is for general experience, not specific past events. Use 了 instead: 我昨天去了台北。
過 cannot be used with frequency adverbs like 常常 (often). Use the simple verb instead: 我常常吃滷肉飯。

incorrect_examples.error: The word order is wrong. 過 must come after the verb, not before. Correct: 我看過電影。
Double 過 is incorrect. Use 沒有 + verb + 過: 他沒有去過台北。

repair_feedback.vocabulary: Use 過 with action verbs like 去, 吃, 喝, 看, 聽, 學, 做.

pattern: Subject + 沒有 + Verb + 過 + Object
explanation_en: Use 沒有 + verb + 過 to express that someone has never done something before. This is the negative form of the experience 過 construction.
title: Negating Past Experience with 沒有...過
meaning: Have never done something before
when_to_use: When saying you have never done something
When answering 'no' to a 過 question
When expressing lack of experience
when_not_to_use: Do not use 不 instead of 沒有 for this pattern
Do not use 過 with 了 in the same sentence
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 從來沒有 + verb + 過 is a stronger way to say 'never ever'. For example: 我從來沒有去過花蓮。This is common in casual speech.
word_order_notes: Place 沒有 before the verb, and 過 after the verb. The word order is: Subject + 沒有 + Verb + 過 + Object.
english_contrast: In English, we say 'I have never been to Taipei.' In Chinese, 沒有 negates the experience, and 過 marks the verb. The structure is different: 'I not have go over Taipei.'
korean_contrast: In Korean, the equivalent is '-아/어 본 적이 없다' (e.g., '저는 타이베이에 가 본 적이 없어요'). 沒有...過 is more direct and common in spoken Chinese.

pattern: 以前 + Subject + Verb + 過 + Object
explanation_en: Use 以前 (yǐqián) at the beginning of a sentence to indicate that something happened in the past, often combined with 過 to emphasize that it was an experience. 以前 means 'before' or 'in the past'.
title: Talking About Past Experiences with 以前
meaning: In the past, I have done something
when_to_use: When introducing a past experience without a specific time
When comparing past and present experiences
When telling stories about what you did before
when_not_to_use: Do not use 以前 with specific time words like 昨天 or 上個星期 (use those alone)
Do not use 以前 for future plans
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 以前 is very common in storytelling and casual conversation. It can be used with or without 過. For example: 以前我去過台北。or 以前我住在台北。Both are natural.
word_order_notes: 以前 usually comes at the beginning of the sentence, before the subject. The word order is: 以前 + Subject + Verb + 過 + Object.
english_contrast: In English, we say 'Before, I have been to Taipei.' In Chinese, 以前 sets the time frame, and 過 marks the experience. The structure is similar but 以前 is more flexible.
korean_contrast: In Korean, the equivalent is '예전에' or '전에' (e.g., '예전에 저는 타이베이에 가 본 적이 있어요'). 以前 is used similarly at the beginning of the sentence.

drill_examples.translation_en: I have been to the National Palace Museum in Taipei before.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 저는 예전에 타이베이의 고궁에 가 본 적이 있습니다.

drill_examples.text: 你以前吃過臺灣的小吃嗎？
drill_examples.pinyin: Nǐ yǐqián chī guò Táiwān de xiǎochī ma?
drill_examples.translation_en: Have you ever eaten Taiwanese street food before?
drill_examples.translation_ko: 예전에 대만의 길거리 음식을 먹어 본 적이 있나요?

drill_examples.text: 我從來沒有去過國外旅行。
drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ cónglái méiyǒu qù guò guówài lǚxíng.
drill_examples.translation_en: I have never traveled abroad before.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 저는 예전에 해외 여행을 가 본 적이 전혀 없습니다.

drill_examples.text: 他以前在臺中吃過很好吃的牛肉麵。
drill_examples.pinyin: Tā yǐqián zài Táizhōng chī guò hěn hǎochī de niúròumiàn.
drill_examples.translation_en: He has eaten very delicious beef noodles in Taichung before.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 그는 예전에 타이중에서 아주 맛있는 소고기 국수를 먹어 본 적이 있습니다.

drill_examples.text: 我們以前去過墾丁的海邊，風景很漂亮。
drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒmen yǐqián qù guò Kěndīng de hǎibiān, fēngjǐng hěn piàoliang.
drill_examples.translation_en: We have been to the beach in Kenting before, and the scenery was very beautiful.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 우리는 예전에 컨딩의 해변에 가 본 적이 있는데, 경치가 아주 아름다웠습니다.

drill_examples.text: 你以前喝過珍珠奶茶嗎？
drill_examples.pinyin: Nǐ yǐqián hē guò zhēnzhū nǎichá ma?
drill_examples.translation_en: Have you ever drunk bubble tea before?
drill_examples.translation_ko: 예전에 버블티를 마셔 본 적이 있나요?

drill_examples.text: 我聽過這首歌，可是沒有聽過這個版本。
drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ tīng guò zhè shǒu gē, kěshì méiyǒu tīng guò zhège bǎnběn.
drill_examples.translation_en: I have heard this song before, but I haven't heard this version.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 저는 이 노래를 들어 본 적이 있지만, 이 버전은 들어 본 적이 없습니다.

drill_examples.text: 她以前學過中文，所以會說一點。
drill_examples.pinyin: Tā yǐqián xué guò Zhōngwén, suǒyǐ huì shuō yīdiǎn.
drill_examples.translation_en: She has studied Chinese before, so she can speak a little.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 그녀는 예전에 중국어를 배운 적이 있어서 조금 말할 수 있습니다.

negative_examples.error: Do not use 以前 with a specific time like 昨天. Use either 以前 or 昨天, not both. Correct: 我昨天去過台北。or 以前我去過台北。
This sentence is actually correct! But note: 以前 can be used with negative 沒有...過 to mean 'I have never been to Taipei before.'

incorrect_examples.error: Do not use 了 with 過 when 以前 is used. 了 indicates completion, which conflicts with the general experience meaning. Correct: 我以前去過台北。
Double 過 is incorrect. Use only one 過 after the verb. Correct: 以前我去過台北。

transformation_drills: Add 以前 to '我去過台北': 以前我去過台北。
Change '以前我吃過滷肉飯' to a negative sentence: 以前我沒有吃過滷肉飯。
production_drills: Tell your partner: 以前我去過淡水。
Ask your partner: 以前你有沒有去過九份？
common_error_patterns: Using 以前 with specific time words
Using 了 with 過 in the same sentence

repair_feedback.word_order: Place 以前 at the beginning of the sentence, before the subject.
repair_feedback.politeness: Using 以前 is neutral and polite. No special adjustment needed.
repair_feedback.vocabulary: Use 以前 with 過 verbs like 去, 吃, 喝, 看, 聽, 學, 做.

explanation_en: Use '正在' to emphasize that an action is in progress right now. It's like saying 'in the middle of doing something'.
title: Emphasizing Ongoing Action with 正在
meaning: Indicates an action is actively happening at this moment.
when_to_use: When you want to stress that someone is currently doing something
When answering 'what are you doing?' with focus on the ongoing nature
when_not_to_use: For habitual actions (use 每天/常常 instead)
For future plans (use 要/打算 instead)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 正在 is common in both spoken and written Chinese. It adds a sense of 'right in the middle of' and is slightly more formal than just 在.
word_order_notes: 正在 comes directly after the subject and before the verb. No time words can interrupt this structure.
english_contrast: English uses 'am/is/are -ing' for ongoing actions. 正在 is similar but more emphatic than just 在.
korean_contrast: Korean uses '~고 있다' for ongoing actions. 正在 is like '~고 있는 중이다' (in the middle of doing).

correct_examples.translation_en: What are you doing right now?
She is scrolling on her phone.
Are you doing your homework right now?
He is talking on the phone.

drill_examples.translation_en: What are you doing right now?
She is scrolling on her phone.
Are you doing your homework right now?
He is talking on the phone.

negative_examples.error: 正在 must come before the verb, not after. Correct: 我正在吃飯。
正在 is only for actions, not states like '是'. Use 他現在是學生。

incorrect_examples.error: 正在 is for right now, not habitual actions. Use 我每天看書。
正在 cannot be used for future. Use 他明天要去台北。

transformation_drills: Change '他看書' to emphasize ongoing action: 他正在看書。
Change '我吃飯' to emphasize right now: 我正在吃飯。
production_drills: Answer: 你現在做什麼？(Use 正在)
Describe what your friend is doing right now using 正在
common_error_patterns: Putting 正在 after the verb
Using 正在 with stative verbs like 是, 有, 喜歡

repair_feedback.word_order: Remember: Subject + 正在 + Verb + Object. 正在 always before the verb.
repair_feedback.politeness: Using 正在 is neutral; for polite interruption, add 不好意思 before asking.
repair_feedback.vocabulary: Use action verbs with 正在, not state verbs.

explanation_en: Use '在' to indicate an action is in progress. It's the most common way to express ongoing actions in daily conversation.
title: Ongoing Action with 在
meaning: Shows that someone is doing something right now.
when_to_use: When asking what someone is doing
When describing current activities casually
When telling someone you're busy
when_not_to_use: For completed actions (use 了)
For future actions (use 要/會)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 在 is very common in spoken Chinese. It's less emphatic than 正在 and is the go-to structure for 'what are you doing?' questions.
word_order_notes: 在 comes after the subject and before the verb. It can be used with time words like 現在 at the beginning of the sentence.
english_contrast: English uses 'am/is/are -ing'. 在 is the direct equivalent, e.g., 'I am eating' = '我在吃'.
korean_contrast: Korean uses '~고 있다'. 在 is similar to this structure, e.g., '나는 먹고 있어' = '我在吃'.

correct_examples.translation_en: She is scrolling on her phone.

correct_examples.text: 他正在開會，等一下回你。
correct_examples.pinyin: Tā zhèngzài kāihuì, děng yīxià huí nǐ.
correct_examples.translation_en: He is in a meeting; he'll get back to you in a moment.
We are eating at a restaurant.

drill_examples.translation_en: She is scrolling on her phone.

drill_examples.text: 他正在開會，等一下回你。
drill_examples.pinyin: Tā zhèngzài kāihuì, děng yīxià huí nǐ.
drill_examples.translation_en: He is in a meeting; he'll get back to you in a moment.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 그는 회의 중이에요. 잠시 후에 답장할게요.

drill_examples.translation_en: We are eating at a restaurant.

negative_examples.error: Negation uses 沒有, not 不. Correct: 我沒有在看書。
Negation of 在 is 沒有在. Correct: 他沒有在吃飯。

incorrect_examples.error: 在 is for present ongoing. For past ongoing, use 昨天在 or 那時候在. But 在 alone implies now.
在 is not for habits. Use 他每天跑步。

transformation_drills: Change '他看書' to ongoing: 他在看書。
Change '我吃飯' to ongoing: 我在吃飯。
production_drills: Answer: 你現在做什麼？(Use 在)
Ask a friend what they are doing using 在
common_error_patterns: Using 不 for negation of 在
Using 在 for habitual actions

repair_feedback.word_order: 在 goes before the verb: Subject + 在 + Verb.
repair_feedback.politeness: When asking someone what they are doing, start with 不好意思 or 請問 to be polite.
repair_feedback.vocabulary: Use action verbs like 吃, 看, 寫, 做 with 在.

explanation_en: Use '在忙' to say someone is busy doing something. It's a common way to explain why you can't do something right now.
title: Expressing Being Busy with 在忙
meaning: Indicates that someone is busy with an activity.
when_to_use: When you are busy and cannot talk or help
When explaining why you can't do something now
When asking if someone is free
when_not_to_use: For general statements about being busy (use 很忙)
For past busyness (use 那時候在忙)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 在忙 is very common in daily conversation. It's often used with 不好意思 to politely decline or interrupt. For example: 不好意思，我現在在忙。
word_order_notes: 在忙 can be used alone or followed by a verb phrase. For example: 我在忙工作 or 我在忙著寫功課.
english_contrast: English says 'I'm busy' or 'I'm busy doing something'. 在忙 covers both, e.g., '我在忙' = 'I'm busy', '我在忙工作' = 'I'm busy with work'.
korean_contrast: Korean uses '바쁘다' for busy. 在忙 is like '바쁘게 ~하고 있다' (busy doing something).

correct_examples.translation_en: She is scrolling on her phone.

correct_examples.text: 我現在在忙，等一下再回你訊息。
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ xiànzài zài máng, děng yīxià zài huí nǐ xùnxí.
correct_examples.translation_en: I am busy now, I'll reply to your message later.

correct_examples.pinyin: Bù hǎoyìsi, wǒ xiànzài zài máng.
correct_examples.translation_en: Sorry, I am busy right now.

correct_examples.text: 他正在開會，不方便講電話。
correct_examples.pinyin: Tā zhèngzài kāihuì, bù fāngbiàn jiǎng diànhuà.
correct_examples.translation_en: He is in a meeting and not available to talk on the phone.

drill_examples.translation_en: She is scrolling on her phone.

drill_examples.text: 我現在在忙，等一下再回你訊息。
drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ xiànzài zài máng, děng yīxià zài huí nǐ xùnxí.
drill_examples.translation_en: I am busy now, I'll reply to your message later.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 지금 바빠서, 잠시 후에 메시지 답장할게요.

drill_examples.translation_ko: 그는 회의 중이라 전화를 받기 어려워요.

negative_examples.error: This is correct for 'I'm not busy'. But if you mean 'I'm not busy doing something', use 我沒有在忙什麼。
This is correct for past. But if you mean 'he was busy yesterday', it's fine. No error here.

incorrect_examples.error: Word order is wrong. Use 我在忙吃飯 or 我在忙著吃飯。
Negation of 在忙 is 沒有在忙. Correct: 他沒有在忙。

transformation_drills: Change '我忙' to ongoing: 我在忙。
Change '他工作' to busy with work: 他在忙工作。
production_drills: Answer: 你現在有空嗎？(Say you are busy)
Explain why you can't talk now using 在忙
common_error_patterns: Using 不 instead of 沒有 for negation
Putting 忙 after the verb instead of before

repair_feedback.word_order: 在忙 comes before the verb phrase: Subject + 在忙 + (Verb + Object).
repair_feedback.politeness: When saying you're busy, add 不好意思 to be polite: 不好意思，我現在在忙。
repair_feedback.vocabulary: Use 在忙 with activities like 工作, 寫功課, 開會.

explanation_en: Use 會 to express ability or skill, meaning 'can' or 'know how to'. It indicates that you have learned or acquired the ability to do something.
title: Expressing Ability with 會
meaning: Indicates that someone knows how to do something or has the skill to do it.
when_to_use: When talking about skills you have learned, like languages, sports, or cooking
When asking if someone can do something
When describing your abilities in a positive way
when_not_to_use: Do not use 會 for temporary possibility (use 可以 instead)
Do not use 會 for future plans (use 要 or 打算 instead)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 會 is commonly used in daily conversation to talk about skills. It's polite to ask '你會說中文嗎？' when meeting new people.
word_order_notes: 會 comes directly after the subject and before the verb. The object follows the verb.
english_contrast: In English, 'can' is used for both ability and permission. In Chinese, 會 is only for learned ability, not permission.
korean_contrast: In Korean, '-(으)ㄹ 수 있다' is used for ability, similar to 會. However, 會 specifically implies learned skill, while Korean can also use '할 줄 알다' for learned ability.

correct_examples.pinyin: Tā bù tài huì xiě zì, kěshì hěn nǔlì liànxí.

repair_feedback.politeness: Using 會 in questions is polite and natural.
repair_feedback.vocabulary: Use 會 only for learned abilities, not for temporary situations.

pattern: Subject + 不 會 + Verb + Object
explanation_en: Use 不 會 to express that you do not know how to do something or lack the ability. It is the negative form of 會.
title: Negating Ability with 不 會
meaning: Indicates that someone does not know how to do something or lacks the skill.
when_to_use: When you want to say you cannot do something because you haven't learned it
When answering negatively to a question about ability
When describing what you are not capable of
when_not_to_use: Do not use 不 會 for things that are impossible due to external reasons (use 不能 instead)
Do not use 不 會 for things you don't want to do (use 不想 instead)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, it's common to say '我 不 會 說 中文' when you are a beginner. It's polite to add '還 在 學' to show you are learning.
word_order_notes: 不 會 comes directly after the subject and before the verb. The object follows the verb.
english_contrast: In English, 'I can't' can mean both lack of ability and impossibility. In Chinese, 不 會 is specifically for lack of learned ability.
korean_contrast: In Korean, '못 하다' or '할 줄 모르다' is used for lack of ability, similar to 不 會. However, 不 會 is more specific to learned skills.

correct_examples.text: 我不會說中文，可是我想慢慢學。

pattern: Subject + 還 不 太 會 + Verb + Object
structure: 還 不 太 會
explanation_en: Use 還 不 太 會 to express that you are still learning and not yet proficient. It indicates partial ability or ongoing learning.
title: Expressing Partial Ability with 還 不 太 會
meaning: Indicates that someone is still learning and cannot do something well yet.
when_to_use: When you are a beginner and still learning a skill
When you want to be humble about your ability
When describing progress in learning
when_not_to_use: Do not use 還 不 太 會 if you are already proficient (use 會 instead)
Do not use 還 不 太 會 for things you have never tried (use 不 會 instead)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, saying '我 還 不 太 會 說 中文' is a humble and polite way to indicate you are learning. It encourages others to be patient and helpful.
word_order_notes: 還 不 太 會 is a fixed phrase that comes after the subject and before the verb. The object follows the verb.
english_contrast: In English, 'I'm not very good at it yet' conveys a similar meaning. 還 不 太 會 is more specific to ongoing learning.
korean_contrast: In Korean, '아직 잘 못 해요' is similar, meaning 'not yet good at it'. Both imply ongoing learning and humility.

pattern: Subject + 長得 / 看起來 + Adjective
explanation_en: Use 長得 to describe inherent physical traits (height, build, etc.) and 看起來 for perceived appearance or impression.
title: Describing Appearance with 長得 and 看起來
meaning: Describing how someone looks
when_to_use: Describing height, build, or facial features
Giving first impressions of someone's appearance
when_not_to_use: Describing personality (use 個性 instead)
Describing temporary states like clothing (use 穿著)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 長得 is common for physical traits; 看起來 is softer and often used for compliments like '你看起來很年輕'.
word_order_notes: Adjective always comes after 長得 or 看起來, never before.
english_contrast: English says 'He is tall' directly; Chinese requires 長得 before the adjective.
korean_contrast: Korean uses '~이/가 키가 크다' structure; Chinese uses 長得 + adjective.

explanation_en: Use 很 before adjectives to describe personality traits. 很 is neutral here, not meaning 'very'.
title: Describing Personality with 很 + Adjective
meaning: Describing someone's personality
when_to_use: Describing character traits like 友善, 幽默, 認真
Making general statements about someone's nature
when_not_to_use: Describing physical appearance (use 長得)
Emphasizing 'very' (use 非常 or 很 with stress)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 很 is often used as a linking verb, not intensifier. Saying '他友善' sounds incomplete; '他很友善' is natural.
word_order_notes: Adjective always follows 很, never before the subject.
english_contrast: English says 'He is friendly'; Chinese requires 很 before the adjective: '他很友善'.
korean_contrast: Korean uses '그는 친절해요' without a separate word; Chinese needs 很.

pattern: 那個人 + 是 + 誰 / 我朋友 / 他
explanation_en: Use 那個人 to refer to a specific person, then identify them with 是.
title: Identifying People with 那個人
meaning: Identifying a specific person
when_to_use: Pointing out someone in a group
Asking or answering who someone is
when_not_to_use: Describing appearance (use 長得)
Talking about yourself (use 我)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 那個人 is common in casual conversation. For politeness, add 請問 before questions.
word_order_notes: 那個人 comes first, then 是, then the identifier.
english_contrast: English says 'That person is my friend'; Chinese order is same: 那個人是我朋友.
korean_contrast: Korean uses '그 사람은 내 친구예요'; Chinese uses 那個人 + 是.

explanation_en: Use '比' to compare two items, stating that A is more [adjective] than B.
title: Basic Comparison with 比
meaning: A is more [adjective] than B
when_to_use: Comparing prices, sizes, distances, or qualities of two items
Stating a clear difference between two options
when_not_to_use: When the difference is small or uncertain
When comparing more than two items at once
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, '比' is very common in daily conversation, especially when discussing food or shopping choices. It's neutral and polite.
word_order_notes: The structure is fixed: A + 比 + B + adjective. Do not add '很' or other degree adverbs before the adjective.
english_contrast: In English, we say 'A is more [adjective] than B'. In Chinese, the word order is A + 比 + B + adjective, without 'more'.
korean_contrast: In Korean, you use 'A가 B보다 더 [adjective]' (A-ga B-boda deo [adjective]). The Chinese structure is similar but without '더' (deo).

values.meaning_en: almost the same; about the same; not much difference

pattern: A 跟 B 差不多
explanation_en: Use '跟...差不多' to say that two items are similar or almost the same in a certain aspect.
title: Similarity with 差不多
meaning: A and B are about the same
when_to_use: When two items have similar prices, sizes, or qualities
When you want to say there's no big difference
when_not_to_use: When there is a clear difference
When you want to emphasize one is better
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, '差不多' is very common in casual conversation, especially when comparing food or prices. It's a soft way to say 'no big difference'.
word_order_notes: The structure is: A + 跟 + B + 差不多. You can add a noun after '差不多' if needed, e.g., 'A 跟 B 差不多貴'.
english_contrast: In English, we say 'A and B are about the same'. In Chinese, the structure is A + 跟 + B + 差不多.
korean_contrast: In Korean, you use 'A와 B가 비슷하다' (A-wa B-ga biseuthada). The Chinese structure is similar but uses '差不多' instead of '비슷하다'.

repair_feedback.word_order: Remember: A + 跟 + B + 差不多 + (adjective), no '很'.

explanation_en: Use '沒有' to express that A is not as [adjective] as B.
title: Negative Comparison with 沒有
meaning: A is not as [adjective] as B
when_to_use: When you want to say A is less [adjective] than B
When comparing qualities like price, size, or distance
when_not_to_use: When A is more [adjective] than B (use 比 instead)
When the comparison is about equality (use 差不多 instead)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, '沒有' is commonly used in negative comparisons, especially when discussing prices or convenience. It's polite and clear.
word_order_notes: The structure is: A + 沒有 + B + adjective. Do not add '很' before the adjective.
english_contrast: In English, we say 'A is not as [adjective] as B'. In Chinese, the structure is A + 沒有 + B + adjective.
korean_contrast: In Korean, you use 'A가 B만큼 [adjective]하지 않다' (A-ga B-mankeum [adjective]haji anta). The Chinese structure is simpler with '沒有'.
